coredata.dc:title: Activities of BiFeO3/carbon-dots catalysts in piezo-photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin upon light/ultrasonic excitation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiaojian
Zhang, Zhiqin
Long, Kexin
Yuan, Honglei
Sun, Xianke
coredata.dc:description: Designing catalysts that can effectively make use of renewable energy benefits to solve the current challenges of environmental pollution and increasing energy demands. Piezo-photocatalysis that can utilize solar energy and natural vibration-energy has emerged as a “green” technique. In this work, we fabricated BiFeO3/C nano composites that can harvest solar and vibration energies and degrade organic pollutants. The incorporated carbon quantum dots bring about more efficient visible light absorbance and separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The piezoelectric polarization further suppresses the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The catalysts own higher reaction rates in piezo-photocatalysis and the BiFeO3/C-0.12 shows the highest degradation efficiency (k-value of 0.0835 min−1).
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiFeO3/C
Nano particles
Piezo-photocatalysis
Ciprofloxacin degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns and Raman spectra of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12.
SEM (A), TEM (B and E), HRTEM (C), SAED pattern (D) and elemental distribution (F-I) images of the BiFeO3/C-0.12.
XPS spectra of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12.
Phase-voltage hysteresis loop (A), amplitude-voltage (B) and M−H (C)curves of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12.
Catalytic performance of the fabricated samples in photocatalysis (A) and piezo-photocatalysis (B).
Reaction rate constants of the fabricated samples in photocatalysis (A) and piezo-photocatalysis (B).
Influence of catalyst concentration (A), ciprofloxacin concentration (B) and ultrasonic frequency (C) on piezo-photocatalytic performance.
N2 desorption/adsorption isotherms of the fabricated samples.
Light absorbance spectra (A) and plots of (αhν)2 vs hν (B) for the fabricated samples.
Photocurrent responses under light irradiation (A) and light/ultrasonic irradiation (B) of the fabricated samples.
Trapping experiment results (A), ESR curves of DMPO-•OH (B) and DMPO-•O2 − (C).
Band structures of the fabricated samples and possible degradation mechanism.
Eg, EVB and ECB of BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C.

head.ce:title: Activities of BiFeO3/carbon-dots catalysts in piezo-photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin upon light/ultrasonic excitation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Telecommunication Engineering, Zhoukou Normal University, Zhoukou, 466001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing catalysts that can effectively make use of renewable energy benefits to solve the current challenges of environmental pollution and increasing energy demands. Piezo-photocatalysis that can utilize solar energy and natural vibration-energy has emerged as a “green” technique. In this work, we fabricated BiFeO3/C nano composites that can harvest solar and vibration energies and degrade organic pollutants. The incorporated carbon quantum dots bring about more efficient visible light absorbance and separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The piezoelectric polarization further suppresses the recombination of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. The catalysts own higher reaction rates in piezo-photocatalysis and the BiFeO3/C-0.12 shows the highest degradation efficiency (k-value of 0.0835 min−1).

ce:section.ce:para:
It is a general view that environmental crisis threatens to human's existence and becomes more and more serious following the development of modern society. Various pollutants exist in water, soil, atmosphere and damage human health. For example, antibiotics, as a type of significant curative medicine to diseases induced by bacterial and pathogen infections, have been found that they also cause harm to human beings (i.e., the inhibition of bone growth in children) on account of their indiscriminate uses and survival in environment [1–3]. In order to degrade the pollutants, researchers all over the world devote themselves to explore “green” techniques, which use sustainable energy and don’t cause new pollution. Photocatalysis is one of closed watched “green” techniques because that it can use solar energy to degrade pollutants. In a photocatalysis process, photons with higher energy than the band gap energy of photocatalyst is absorbed, which leads to the promotions of electrons from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) and gives rise to the production of holes in VB [4]. As a result, the photoinduced electron-hole (
e
-
-
h
+
) pairs are generated upon the light irradiation. And if the
e
-
-
h
+
pairs have enough activities, active species, such as hydroxyl radical (•OH), superoxide radical (
·
O 2 -
), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), will be generated. These active species react with pollutants and degrade them to non-toxic products. Piezocatalysis also is a notable “green” technique, which can degrade pollutants by converting natural vibration-energy into electrochemical energy in a reversible manner [5]. Depending on the stress, polarization is formed in the piezoelectric materials, which brings about an equal number of positive/negative charge carriers [6]. The generated positive/negative charge carriers lead to redox reactions by reacting with O2 and OH− in the solution to generate active species. Meanwhile, it is worth noting that most of piezoelectric materials are semiconductors. It means that they can also act as photocatalysts to degrade pollutants by absorbing light energy. Then, professor Wang reported the piezo-photocatalysis in 2010 by coupling the piezopotential and light energy absorbance in semiconductors [7]. In which, the piezopotential offers a long-range order driving force for separation of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs. So far, some piezoelectric materials have been reported and shown excellent piezo-photocatalytic activities, such as KTa0.5Nb0.5O3 [8], BaTiO3 [9], Bi5O7I [10], CdxZn1-xS [11], Bi4Ti3O12 [12], Na0.5Bi0.5TiO3 [13], and BiFeO3 [14,15].

On account of excellent catalytic ability depending on synergetic piezo-photocatalytic effect, piezo-photocatalysis is paid more and more attention. For a piezo-photocatalytic material, it is desirable that it has excellent polarization and narrow band gap (Eg). BiFeO3 is one of typical ABO3-type perovskite materials and belongs to R3c space group, which owns a high piezoelectric coefficient (d33) about 100 pmV and a relatively narrow Eg [16,17]. As a result, BiFeO3 is one of suitable piezo-photocatalytic materials. The exceptional piezoelectric effect benefits to restrict fast recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs and thus leads to high degradation efficiency. The deficiencies that restrict its practical applications in catalytic field are its limited active sites and quick recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs [18,19]. For obtaining more active sites, the widely used methods are size and morphological control [18,20,21], as well as integration with organic materials [22–24]. For suppressing recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs, a widely used method is to form heterojunction structure with other materials [17,19,22,25–28].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a group of neoteric carbon nano materials with sizes in the range of 2–10 nm, are widely used in photocatalysis on account of their unique chemical and optical characteristics [29,30]. Lots of CQDs decorated semiconductors have been reported and show excellent photocatalytic performance [31–34]. In one hand, CQDs can enhance visible light absorbance of photocatalysts. In the other hand, CQDs can enhance adsorption capacity of the photocatalysts. Herein, we give the design and fabrication of novel CQDs decorated BiFeO3 nano particles using an in-situ solvothermal route. The concentration of glucose during the fabrication was controlled carefully for optimizing the design of composites. CQDs enhance the visible light absorbance and provide more active sites, which results in high performance in catalytic processes. The catalytic activities in photocatalysis, piezocatalysis and piezo-photocatalysis were investigated in detail. A possible mechanism of the different activities in different catalytic processes was speculated on the basis of experiment results.

At first, BiFeO3 nano particles were fabricated using a solvothermal route. The reagents of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (99.9 %) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (99.9 %) were used as starting materials. The mixture of deionized water and ethanol with a volume ratio of 1:5 was used as the solvent. PEG 6000 was used as the surface-active agent. In a typical fabrication, 1.0 g of PEG 6000 was dissolved in 30 mL of mixed solvent under magnetic stirring for 30 min (solution A). Subsequently, 2.425 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O and 2.020 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were dissolved in 15 mL of 60 % HNO3 solution under magnetic stirring for 15 min to obtain a metal complex solution (solution B). Next, the solution B was added to the solution A under magnetic stirring for 30 min (solution C). Further, 10 g of NaOH was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring for 15 min and then was added to solution C under magnetic stirring for 30 min. The final brown suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethyl alcohol for 5 times, and dried at 60 °C for 20 h. Finally, the as-prepared samples were annealed at 550 °C for 2 h in atmosphere and the taupe BiFeO3 catalysts were obtained.

Next, CQDs decorated BiFeO3 nano particles were fabricated using a solvothermal route. In a typical fabrication, 0.06 g of BiFeO3 nano particles was added into 60 mL of glucose solution (0.12 mM) under magnetic stirring for 30 min. Subsequently, the milky suspension was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (80 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The product was collected by centrifugation, washed with ethyl alcohol for 5 times, and dried at 60 °C in vacuum. Herein, the taupe BiFeO3/C catalysts were obtained. In order to investigate influence of CQDs content on performance, the glucose solution with concentrations of 0.04, 0.08, 0.12 and 0.16 mM was used respectively in the fabrication. The corresponding products were labeled as BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.12 and BiFeO3/C-0.16, respectively. Pure CQDs were fabricated via the similar procedure without the addition of BiFeO3.

The measurements of X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman, scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM (HRTEM), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), selected area electron diffraction (SAED), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), piezoelectricity, magnetism, specific surface area, UV–visible absorbance, photocurrent response, electron spin resonance (ESR), emission, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and catalytic activity were carried out. The details of the measurements were shown in the Supporting Materials.

XRD patterns of the fabricated BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 nano particles are provided by Fig. 1 A. In order to determine the phase purity, the standard diffraction lines of BiFeO3 are also provided in Fig. 1A. The diffraction patterns of other samples are similar with them. By comparing the standard date with the experimental data, we can find that the diffraction lines of the fabricated samples are well according with the standard XRD data. It demonstrates that the fabricated BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C nano particles have the pure phase. The diffraction lines corresponding to CQDs aren’t observed clearly in XRD patterns of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 sample. But the Raman spectrum provided in Fig. 1B shows the Raman-active modes corresponding to carbon. In the Raman spectrum of BiFeO3/C-0.12, two peaks in the rage of 1200–1650 cm−1 respectively correspond to the D-band and G-band of carbon, which confirms the existence of carbon [34]. On the basis of group theory, the Raman-active modes BiFeO3 belonging to the R3c space group could be given as
Γ
Raman
= 4 A 1 + 9 E
, where A1 and E modes respectively correspond to Bi and Fe ions in BiFeO3 [35]. As shown in Fig. 1B, the fabricated samples present six Raman-active modes among thirteen theoretical modes including four A1 modes and two E modes. These results suggest successful fabrication of BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C composites.

The SEM and TEM images of the synthesized BiFeO3 are provided in Fig. S1, indicating that the incorporated CQDs do not obviously influence the morphology and size of BiFeO3 nanoparticles. The SEM and TEM images of the synthesized carbon samples are also shown in Fig. S1. Fig. 2 provides the SEM, TEM, HRTEM, SAED and elemental distribution images of the fabricated BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst. Fig. 2A provides a SEM image of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst, showing that lots of particles with irregular morphologies are formed. Fig. 2B provides a TEM image of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst, showing that tiny particles embed into the surface of irregular particles. As shown in the HRTEM image shown in Fig. 2C, the lattice spacings with 0.327 and 0.396 nm are according with the (002) lattice plane of carbon [36] and (012) lattice plane of BiFeO3 [37]. Fig. 2D provides the SAED pattern of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst, the four intense rings in it can be indexed as (012), (104), (113) and (024) lattice planes of rhombohedral BiFeO3. Fig. 4E-4I provide SEM and corresponding elemental distribution images of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst. Clearly, the elements of Bi, Fe, O and C exist and distribute equably. These results also confirm the successful fabrication of BiFeO3/C composites and in-situ growth of CQDs on the surface of BiFeO3. Fig. S2 provides EDS spectra of the synthesized BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C catalysts. On the basis of the EDS data, the approximate C contents for the BiFeO3/C catalysts are obtained. The C contents for the BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.12 and BiFeO3/C-0.16 catalysts are about 0.13 wt%, 0.25 wt%, 0.34 wt% and 0.40 wt%, respectively.

The chemical states and elements of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts were further inspected using XPS analysis. Fig. 3
A provides the survey XPS spectra of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts, which clearly show signals of Bi, Fe, O and C. Fig. 3B and 3C provide high resolution XPS spectra of Bi 4f and Fe 2p for the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts. For Bi 4f, two peaks appear at 158.8/159.0 eV and 164.0/164.2 eV for the BiFeO3/BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts, which correspond to the Bi 4f7/2/4f5/2, respectively [8]. For Fe 2p, two peaks appear at 710.7/710.9 eV and 724.5/724.7 eV for the BiFeO3/BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts, which correspond to the Fe 2p3/2/2p1/2, respectively [17]. The binding energy peaks of Bi 4f and Fe 2p for the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst shift to larger values comparing with the BiFeO3. It is induced probably by the interaction between CODs and BiFeO3. Fig. 3D provides high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s for the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts. The peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to the compensated carbon from the XPS instrument during the test. For the BiFeO3/C-0.12, the peaks at 286.2 eV and 288.4 eV correspond to C-O and sp2-hybridized carbon, respectively [36]. The XPS results, along with the XRD, Raman, SEM and TEM studies, demonstrate the existence of CQDs in BiFeO3.

For investigating piezoelectric effect of the fabricated catalysts under ultrasonic stress, the localized piezoelectric signals of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts were revealed by the piezoresponse force microscope (PFM) measurements. Fig. 4A provides the phase-voltage hysteresis loops of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts, which owns a 180° change under a 6 V direct current bias field. It indicates that the fabricated catalysts have the ferroelectric polarization switching [9]. Fig. 4B provides the amplitude-voltage curves of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts, which shows a V-shaped hysteresis loop. It means that the fabricated catalysts have the excellent piezoelectric response [22]. Fig. 4C provides the magnetic-hysteresis (M−H) curves of the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalysts in the range of ± 20 kOe. The results indicate that the fabricated catalysts have weak ferromagnetism at room temperature. The magnetization originates from the incomplete and uncompensated antiferromagnetic spin spiral of nanoparticles size, which will result in the formation of some super exchange antiferromagnetic bonds. This characteristic brings about efficient separation of catalysts from water because these catalysts can appear obvious magnetic response to the external magnetic field [38].

The catalytic activities of the fabricated catalysts were evaluated by degrading ciprofloxacin. Fig. 5 A provides the C/C0 values without and with the presence of catalyst upon simulative sunlight irradiation. The ciprofloxacin concentration barely changes in the photocatalysis process without the presence of catalyst. And the ciprofloxacin concentration decreases highly after 30 min’ adsorption–desorption equilibrium with the presence of catalysts, which is induced by the adsorption of the catalysts. The results indicate that the CQDs have the highest adsorption ability among the fabricated samples. The decorated CQDs can enhance the adsorption ability of the BiFeO3 nano particles and the adsorption ability increases continuously with the increasing concentration of the glucose solution. The ciprofloxacin concentration barely changes following the extension of light irradiation time with the presence of CQDs, indicating that the fabricated CQDs don’t show photocatalytic activity. The ciprofloxacin concentration decreases clearly while BiFeO3 or BiFeO3/C is added and the BiFeO3/C-0.12 shows the highest degradation efficiency among the fabricated samples. The final degradation efficiencies of ciprofloxacin in the photocatalysis with the presence of BiFeO3, BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.12 and BiFeO3/C-0.16 are 35.1 %, 56.8 %, 60.4 %, 67.5 % and 63.7 %, respectively. Fig. 5B provides the C/C0 values without and with the presence of catalyst upon light/ultrasonic irradiation. Obviously, the light/ultrasonic irradiation doesn’t result in degradation of ciprofloxacin without the presence of catalysts and the CQDs don’t show catalytic activity in piezo-photocatalysis either. But for other BiFeO3 and BiFeO3-C samples, the degradation efficiency increases obviously. For BiFeO3, BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.12 and BiFeO3/C-0.16, the final degradation efficiencies in the piezo-photocatalysis are 47.6 %, 81.2 %, 87.3 %, 99.9 % and 94.2 %, respectively. The degradation efficiencies of the fabricated samples under ultrasonic irradiation are provided in Fig. S3. The result suggests that the ciprofloxacin concentration is 79.2 % as that of initial concentration with the presence of CQDs. But it doesn’t mean that the fabricated CQDs show piezoeffect. The decrease of the ciprofloxacin concentration is induced by the adsorption. The final degradation efficiencies enhance following the increasing concentration of the glucose solution. The decorated CQDs enhance the conductivity of the fabricated catalysts, which brings about faster migration of charge carriers induced by piezoelectric effect and thus leads to higher piezocatalytic activity [39]. Table S1 shows a simple comparison of degradation efficiency for ciprofloxacin in reported articles with the current work. The results indicate that the catalyst reported in this work has an excellent catalytic activity in degradation of ciprofloxacin. In addition, the synthesized catalysts show excellent stabilities in catalytic processes. Fig. S4A provides the degradation efficiencies in five piezo-photocatalysis processes with the presence of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst, showing that the final degradation efficiencies in the five cycles barely change. And the XRD pattern of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst after five cycles (Fig. S4B) indicates that the catalytic processes have no influence on the phase.

A quantitative analysis of reaction rate for the different catalysts in catalytic processes is summarized using reaction rate constant (k). It can be obtained using pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics:
(1)
k = ( ln
C 0
C
) / t
where C0 is the ciprofloxacin concentration at time of 0, C is the ciprofloxacin concentration at different reaction times, t is the reaction time. Fig. 6 provides the relations between
ln
C 0
C
and t with the presence of different catalysts in photocatalysis (Fig. 6A) and piezo-photocatalysis (Fig. 6B). The slope of the liner fit line is equal to approximate k value. Form Fig. 6, two interesting phenomena are concluded. First, we can see that the CQDs can enhance the reaction rate in both photocatalysis and piezo-photocatalysis. The k-value for the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst in the photocatalysis is about 2.597 times as that of BiFeO3. And the k-value for the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst in the piezo-photocatalysis is about 8.698 times as that of BiFeO3. Second, the piezoelectric effect enhances reaction rates of the fabricated catalysts. The k-value for the BiFeO3 catalyst in the piezo-photocatalysis is about 1.548 times as that in photocatalysis. And the k-value for the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst in the piezo-photocatalysis is about 5.186 times as that in photocatalysis. The reasons for them will be discussed in the later parts.

The effects of experimental parameter on degradation efficiency were then investigated. Fig. 7 A provides the influence of catalyst’s concentration (BiFeO3/C-0.12) on degradation efficiency in piezo-photocatalysis when the initial ciprofloxacin concentration is 60 mg/L. Clearly, the higher concentration accelerates the degradation of ciprofloxacin, which should be induced by the generation of the larger number of active species. However, the rate and final efficiency are similar when the concentrations of BiFeO3/C-0.12 are 150 and 200 mg/L. The relatively high concentration will induce light scatting and shielding, which leads to the similar rate and efficiency although the concentration of catalyst is different. Thus, the optimized concentration of catalyst in this work is considered as 150 mg/L. Fig. 7B provides the influence of initial ciprofloxacin concentration on degradation efficiency when the concentration of BiFeO3/C-0.12 is 150 mg/L. The final degradation efficiencies are all near to 100 % when the initial ciprofloxacin concentrations are 20, 40 and 60 mg/L. But the degradation efficiency decreases obviously when the initial ciprofloxacin concentration is 80 mg/L. It should be induced by the insufficient active species. Fig. 7C provides the dependence of degradation efficiency on ultrasonic frequency when the concentrations of ciprofloxacin and BiFeO3/C-0.12 are 60 mg/L and 150 mg/L, respectively. Clearly, the degradation efficiency is highest when the ultrasonic frequency is 45 kHz. It is known that the highest piezocatalytic activity of a piezocatalytic material is obtained at its resonance frequency and the resonance frequency of BiFeO3 is 46.5 kHz [40]. The optimal frequency in piezo-photocatalysis is according with the resonance frequency of BiFeO3.

The results of catalytic processes indicate that the decorated CQDs have obvious influence on adsorption ability of the catalysts. In order to make sure of it, the N2 desorption/adsorption isotherms were measured, as shown in Fig. 8 . The CQDs have a largest specific surface area of 58.662 m2/g and the BiFeO3 owns a smallest specific surface area of 16.357 m2/g among the fabricated samples. With the increasing glucose concentration, the specific surface area of sample increases, which respectively are 23.657, 32.319, 41.258 and 50.856 m2/g for BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.12 and BiFeO3/C-0.16 catalysts. A large specific surface area of a catalyst generally means that it has a higher adsorption capacity. This is well according with the experimental results. After 30 min’ adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the ciprofloxacin concentration is lowest with the presence of CQDs, which is induced by its largest adsorption capacity. Following the increase of specific surface area, the adsorption capacity also increases, which benefits to the enhancement of degradation efficiency.

The influence of decorated CQDs on light absorbance performance was then investigated. Fig. 9 A provides the UV–visible absorbance spectra of the fabricated samples. As seen, pure carbon shows a strong photo absorbance in the range of 200–800 nm. Comparing with other samples, pure carbon has the highest light absorbance capacity. The absorbance edge of pure BiFeO3 is about 580 nm, indicating that it has absorbance ability to visible light. The absorbance edges of BiFeO3/C composites show red shifts and the light absorbance intensities within 500–800 nm increase, which is induced by the decorated CQDs [33]. On the basis of light absorbance spectra, the Eg values could be calculated via Kubelka-Munk equation:
(2)
α h ν = A
( h ν -
E g
)
m
In the equation, α, h, ν and A are absorbance coefficient, Planck’s constant, optical frequency and constant, respectively [25]. BiFeO3 is an n-type semiconductor and thus m = 1/2 [41]. Fig. 9B provides the plots of (αhν)2 vs hν. The Eg values of BiFeO3, BiFeO3/C-0.04, BiFeO3/C-0.08, BiFeO3/C-0.012 and BiFeO3/C-0.16 are provided in Table 1 . The decrease of Eg value is induced by the formation of chemical bonding between CQDs and BiFeO3, which leads to the formation of sub-band energy level between conduction band and valence band of BiFeO3 [42].

Photocatalytic has a close relation with the generation of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs. Fig. 10 provides the photocurrent responses of the fabricated samples under light irradiation (A) and light/ultrasonic irradiation (B) in three cycles. For pure carbon, the electrical signal response almost could not be observed, indicating that they cannot exhibit catalytic activity in photocatalysis, piezocatalysis and piezo-photocatalysis. This is according with the results of degradation experiments. For the CQDs decorated BiFeO3 nano particles, the electrical signals show obvious enhancement comparing with pure BiFeO3 and the BiFeO3/C-0.012 sample has the highest value. The electrical signal of the BiFeO3/C-0.012 sample is stronger than that of BiFeO3/C-0.016, indicating that an optimal glucose concentration is needed for obtaining the highest catalytic activity. The CQDs loaded on the surface of BiFeO3 promote the absorbance of visible light and bring about highly efficient generation of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs, which leads to higher photocurrent density. But superabundant CQDs would cause the decrease of the photocurrent because that superfluous CQDs act as recombination centers of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs [39]. As a result, the BiFeO3/C-0.016 owns a lower photocurrent density than that of BiFeO3/C-0.012. It is also can be observed that the electrical signals for the BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C samples under light/ultrasonic irradiation are stronger than that of signals under light irradiation. The enhanced photocurrent density under light/ultrasonic irradiation is induced by the piezoelectric effect. The higher photocurrent density means that more charge carriers are generated, which leads to high degradation efficiency. It is according with the experimental results. The recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs is also revealed by the emission spectra. As shown in Fig. S5, the spectral shapes and peaks of the emission band for the BiFeO3 barely changes after the incorporation of CQDs, but the emission intensity decreases obviously. It means that the recombination of charge carrier pairs in CODs decorated BiFeO3 is reduced [26]. Among all the prepared catalysts, the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst has the lowest emission intensity, indicating the lowest recombination probability of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs in the photocatalyst.

In order to determine which and how reactive species take part in the degradation process, trapping experiments were carried out. Fig. 11 A provides the experimental results for BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 in piezo-photocatalysis by using ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetate acid disodium (EDTA-2Na) and 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ) as scavengers of h+, •OH, e − and •O2 −. For BiFeO3, the degradation efficiency decreases highly when scavengers of IPA and EDTA-2Na are added. It indicates that •OH and e − play prominent roles in the catalytic processes. But for BiFeO3/C-0.12, the influence of IPA and BQ on degradation efficiency is remarkable, meaning that •OH and •O2 − are dominant reactive species in the catalytic processes. In addition, e − also takes part in the catalytic processes since the degradation efficiency also decreases slightly after the addition of EDTA-2Na. The ESR measurements with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide (DMPO) as probes further confirm the reactive species for BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 in catalytic processes. As shown in Fig. 11B, ESR signals of DMPO-•OH are detected with the presence of BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12, suggesting that •OH species are produced upon light/ultrasonic irradiation. But for DMPO-•O2 −, strong ESR signals can be detected only in catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3/C-0.12. These results are according with the trapping results and demonstrate that the prominent reactive species are different for BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12 in catalytic processes.

Theoretically, photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs are generated by semiconductors while they absorb light energies lower than the Eg. Depending on VB potential (EVB) and EB potential (ECB), the following reactions occur or not:
(3)
h +
+
H 2
O → · O H
(4)
e -
+
O 2
→ ·
O 2 -
The values EVB and ECB of a semiconductor could be speculated via the following formulas:
(5)
E
C B
= χ -
E c
- 0.5
E g
(6)
E
V B
=
E
C B
+
E g
In the formulas, χ and Ec are Mulliken electronegativity of a semiconductor and energy of a free electron (∼4.5 eV). For BiFeO3, the χ value is 6.04 eV [43]. The calculated ECB and EVB values of BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C samples are provided in Table 1. The corresponding band structures are shown in Fig. 12 . The EVB values are more positive than the redox potential H2O/•OH (2.37 eV vs. NHE), indicating that reaction (3) occurs. This is according with the trapping and ESR results. The ECB values are not less negative than the standard redox potential of
O 2
/ ·
O 2 -
(-0.33 eV vs. NHE), indicating that reaction (4) should not occur. But the ESR results indicate that reactive species of •O2 − is generated in catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3/C-0.12. It is induced by oxygen-containing function groups on the surface of CQDs, which could migrate electrons to O2 and form •O2 − [44]. The FTIR spectrum shown in Fig. S6 indicates the fabricated carbon owns oxygen-containing function groups indeed.

On the basis of experimental results and band structure, the mechanism of photocatalysis was speculated. Upon light irradiation, electrons migrate to VB from CB and leave holes in VB. For the BiFeO3 catalyst, the photoinduced
e
-
cannot react with dissolved O2 to generate active •O2 − species. Thus,
e
-
and •OH species play the prominent roles in the catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3. For the BiFeO3/C catalysts, on account of remarkable electron reservoir performance and formation of heterojunction between BiFeO3 and CQDs, photoinduced electrons are trapped easily by CQDs. In one hand, it suppresses fast recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs and thus enhances degradation efficiency. In the other hand, as discussed above, it leads to the occurrence of reaction (4) and results in the formation of •O2 − reactive species. Thus, different from the catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3, the •OH and •O2 − reactive species play the prominent roles in the catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3/C.

As shown in Fig. 12, polarization is generated upon ultrasonic irradiation, which induces a certain extent tilt of energy levels to break the electrical equilibrium on the solid–liquid interface [45]. An equal amount of concentrated positive and negative charges are produced on account of the piezoelectric effect. It induces a built-in inside electric field in the catalyst, which leads to the charge separation. The free electrons and holes flow toward the polar surfaces in opposite directions owing to driving of the polarization field. In the piezo-photocatalysis process, on the one hand, the piezoelectric effect leads to the generation of charge carrier. On the other hand, the piezoelectric effect benefits to the isolation of photoproduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs. As a result, the piezo-photocatalysis process owns the highest degradation efficiency.

Herein, BiFeO3 and CQDs decorated BiFeO3 nano particles that own excellent piezo-photocatalytic activity were fabricated successfully using a solvothermal route. The BiFeO3 catalyst shows low degradation efficiency with 35.1 % in one hour in photocatalysis. The incorporation of CQDs enhances the degradation efficiency effectively, with an efficiency of 67.5 % for the BiFeO3/C-0.12 catalyst. The reasons for higher efficiencies of the BiFeO3/C composites are that the decorated CQDs enhance the visible light absorbance and facilitate the migration of electrons. The k-value of the BiFeO3/C-0.12 in photocatalysis is 2.597 times as that of BiFeO3. In the piezo-photocatalysis, the degradation efficiencies are further enhanced, with k-values of 0.0096 and 0.0835 for BiFeO3 and BiFeO3/C-0.12. The reason for the further enhancement of efficiency is that the internal piezoelectric-field-induced bias in BiFeO3 restricts the recombination of photoinduced
e
-
-
h
+
pairs. Trapping experiments demonstrate that •OH and e − play prominent roles in catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3, but •OH and •O2 − are major reactive species in catalytic processes with the presence of BiFeO3/C catalysts. The reason for it is that the oxygen-containing function groups on the surface of CQDs migrate electrons to O2 and form •O2 −.
Xiaojian Zhang: . Zhiqin Zhang: Investigation. Kexin Long: Software, Validation. Honglei Yuan: Data curation. Xianke Sun: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ultsonch.2024.106770.

ce:section.ce:para:
